sentences.text: Oh.
Well, yeah.
We knew about that.
We didn't know actually how far they came.
But the ghetto itself wasn't that terrible, because we were still with our families.
We were with our friends.
And, you know, when you're young, you sleep 20 on the floor in the living room.
It is not such a horrible thing.
It was also summer.
It was middle of May.
The weather was nice.
We had enough to eat, probably not the quality of food that we were used to.
But we could take some food with us, so we didn't really suffer of-- we didn't lack food or anything.
We were-- We were doing OK.
It was a very crowded situation.
But I guess maybe the adults or the older people might have been bothered by that more than us kids.
We were basically outside during the day.
And we were OK.
We were OK because we did not know what is going on.
But as soon as everybody-- well, let's say something about the ghetto.

There was sort of a Jewish, what they called, I think, in Poland Judenrat, a Jewish administration, which of course was really meaningless, because our whole ghetto experience lasted-- mine was maybe less than three weeks.
But even people who got there on May the 3rd, nobody's was more than three weeks, really, because it was so late in the war.
But, anyway, they did establish some kind of a Jewish administration.
And everybody had some kind of a function.
I remember my father had other, or miscellaneous, whatever that was.
There were lots of beatings going on.
I guess we were lucky my father was not beaten.
But a lot of Jewish men were severely beaten, because they did not believe them that they gave all their money, all their jewelry, everything away.
We had to give everything.
Before we got into the ghetto we couldn't even keep a watch, anything.
I think the only thing they let you keep, if I remember correctly, was maybe a wedding ring.
They would let you keep that, but I'm not sure of that.
But everything else, you couldn't keep money.
You couldn't keep jewelry.
You couldn't-- nothing.
I mean, absolutely nothing.
You had to give everything away.
And there were some severe beatings by the Hungarian police, the Hungarian gendarme, which is a section of the police, a branch of the Hungarian police.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The ghetto-- as I said, the ghetto itself wasn't that horrible, because we were told that we are going to be resettled in Germany and we are going to work in factories or on the fields, wherever our labor is needed for the Nazi war economy.
And we were assured that work will be hard, but we will be OK.
We'll be treated well.
Resettlement was the word that were used.
They started taking people away, 3,000 at the time, as soon as we were all in the ghetto area, which was about the middle of May.
Now, [SIGHS] you know, 6 million people, 11 million people all together, were murdered in this period of time.
It is a very large number.
You cannot mourn that many people.
In my case, of course, since I had no siblings, it was basically my parents.
I had one grandparent, a grandmother who was 81 years old, who was a great-grandmother.
She had two seven-year-old great-grandsons.
Actually, at 18, I was her youngest grandchild.
I didn't know the rest of my grandparents.
My parents were-- my mother was the youngest child in her family.
And my father was in the younger generation in his family.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] So two of my grandparents on my mother's side and my grandfather on my father's side died before I was born, so I never knew them.
[SIGHS] About a week before we left, we were-- we were to be the last people to be evacuated since we were the last ones, among the last ones to get into the ghetto area.
Something happened to my mother that decided her fate at an early stage.
In this one kitchen where all these women tried to cook dinner at night, or make something, somebody by accident dropped a tea kettle with boiling water and my mother was standing nearby.
And she burned one foot pretty badly.
Our own family physician was also in the ghetto, and he did take care of her.
But a week later, when we were evacuated, she was still wearing slippers on that foot.
It was healing nicely, but it wasn't completely healed yet.

Since the small hospital that was in the ghetto was also evacuated in the same time we were, my father was very happy because he was able to arrange for my mother to travel with hospital patients and also doctors and their families, I think, that were in those two hospital cars, where there were only 30 people to the same size of cattle car that the rest of us were maybe 150 too.
So my mother would be able to sit down and put her foot up and be a little more comfortable since her foot wasn't still-- you know, her burn wasn't completely healed.
The rest of us were shipped in these cattle cars.
I don't know the exact number, but I think 150 would be about the right number for each one of those cars.

The way it happened, we were standing around in this ghetto area, and they came with some horse-drawn carriages that took out-- we were allowed to take like a little knapsack, a little rucksack with us.
They would take that and the people walked behind them.
I remember standing around with my father.
And the chief of the police, Hungarian police, who was in charge of this operation, was standing with us.
And he was just telling some stories that, ah, oh, you will appreciate us, you know, after you left here, like, if it was our choice, of course, you know to leave.
And I remember my father, who was a very heavy smoker, he was out of cigarettes.
And he sort of jokingly said that I'd give a house for a cigarette.
And I knew this Hungarian chief of police, because he was in charge of organizing the ghetto.
And we worked for him, basically, when we were registering people.
And I guess I was sort of pretty self-confident young person at that time.
And I just turned around and told him-- I don't know what his rank was, captain or something-- would you please offer my father a cigarette?
And I guess he was too embarrassed not to, even though he was a real maniac.
I mean, he was a real vicious anti-Semite.
But he did take out his cigarette case and offer my father a cigarette.
I think I just caught him off guard, sort of and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Anyway, we were crammed into these cattle cars, cattle cars not meant for human transportation, obviously.
We had no windows or no doors open.
And we were sitting on the floor.
But we were so many that I remember everybody couldn't really sit in the same time.
Some people had to stand and some people sat.
We alternated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our journey began-- if I look at a map today, I think it could be done in about maybe 10 to 12 hours, but it took us 3 days.
We left on May the 31st of 1944.
I remember there was a little crack on the wall.
And one time when the train stopped, my father looked out and he could discern the name of the station.
And since he really knew his geography and I guess his railway situation very well, he said that this is a crucial point, because if at this point we're going to turn east, we're going into Poland.
If we turn west, that's good news, because we're going into Germany.
And I think we already had some kind of awareness that the bad camps are in Poland, not in Germany proper.
And we did turn to the east.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very lucky, because once a day our door was opened and we were given some fresh water and the toilet facility was taken away, emptied, and given back to us.
As I later found out, a lot of people were not that lucky, because they travelled for as long as 10 days, 14 days, never being given fresh water or have the door open.
And a lot of dead bodies would be taken off the train.

In my own family, an uncle of mine, an older brother of my mother, who was taken from a different town, also Hungary at that time, but it used to be Czechoslovakia, He Was beaten so badly by the Hungarian police that his dead body was taken off the train in Auschwitz.
I found that out, of course, later after I came back.
Reason, same as they beat people for, because they didn't believe that he gave them all their money, all their jewelry.
He was a lawyer in a small town that used to be Czechoslovakia only probably about 40, 50 kilometers from where we lived.
On June the 3rd, our train stopped.
Our doors were flung open.
And we saw these men in striped garments who opened our doors.
And they were shouting at us to get off the train quickly.
The noise, I think, is something that I will never forget.
You could hear whistles blowing, people shouting in many different languages.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, of course, the sound of running footsteps.
It was really chaotic and it was very, very loud.
Dogs barking, of course.
When we were getting off the train, we wanted to take our little rucksack with us, but we were told that we cannot take anything.
And we were assured that everything is going to be sent to us later.
Also, I remember getting off the train and I had my mother's one shoe in my hand, because I was sure that after three days she will be able to wear shoes now.
And I wanted to run to the front of train, where I knew that her box car was.
I was stopped by an SS soldier.
And when I told him where I'm going, he said, no, you cannot go there right now.
But don't worry about it, because you will see your mother later.
Men and women were separated, so I was separated from my father.
And I found myself in a column of about five or six women, in a row, of about five or six women.
And we were just told to walk ahead.
At the end of these rope was the SS officer, who didn't look old.
And I suppose when you're 18, not old is probably under 30.
He was perfectly attired, and I suppose that was very visible to us in contrast to our miserable appearance after a three-day journey.
His uniform was just perfectly pressed.
His boots were shining.
He had a riding crop, a whip, in his hand.
And he was whistling a tune all the time.
He never uttered a word, but with the movement of his wrist, with his riding crop, he was pointing to the left or to the right to where each of us should go.
Again, we did not know the identity of this monster at that time, but this was Dr.
Josef Mengele, chief medical officer of Auschwitz, responsible for the death of hundreds of thousands of people, and also the experiments that he used, using human beings as guinea pigs.
I was with my next-door neighbor in the same row, I remember.
And she had her very old mother with her that she was sort of holding by her arm.
And I remember them being sent to the left.
And I sort of took a step in that direction, because I wanted to go with somebody I knew, I suppose.
But he did point me to the right.
Going left, as we later found out, of course, was immediate death, was to the gas chamber.
The two box cars with hospital patients had absolutely no chance.
There was no selection.
They were all immediately sent to the left, which, of course, I had no idea about at that point.
Who went to the left?
Who didn't have a chance?
Older people did not have a chance.
Children, of course, did not have a chance.
And mothers with children did not have a chance, because they did not want to create any kind of hysteria by separating them.

Actually, this next-door neighbor of mine, who was a-- a younger woman, in her early thirties, in excellent physical shape, really an athlete, but she was holding her older mother by her arm, so she didn't have a chance, because they wouldn't separate them.
They would just send both of them to the left.
Pregnant women, of course, did not have a chance.

I guess, basically those of us who just at a glance he could tell that he or she is in perfect physical shape even after a miserable journey, and he or she is young enough, strong enough that even under the worst circumstances he can give us some slave labor before he dies or we destroy him, I guess those of us had a chance.
Again, I think, I have to emphasize the date, June the 3rd of 1944, only three days before D-Day.
I suppose the camps were so full that only about 300 of the 3,000 of us did go to the right.
Friends of mine, boys and girls between the ages of 16 and 20, a lot of them never made it to the right for no reason at all, I suppose just sheer arithmetic.
There was no room in the camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were told to just walk to the right.
And I remember there were these barbed wires to our right.
And behind those wires were women.
And they were shouting at us, in our own language, native language, Hungarian, asking us to throw food over the fence if we have any.
Of course, we didn't have anything.
Those barbed wires of course contained high voltage electricity.
Anybody who would touch them would be instantly dead.
But I remember I was with a couple of my girlfriends.

And when we saw these women whose heads were shaved, they had some miserable, filthy piece of garment on, we looked at each other and we were firmly convinced that these women are either insane, or they're criminals, because there we were, probably about 100 to 150 yards on the other side of the tracks.
We could see the smoke coming out of the chimneys.
We could smell something that, of course, we couldn't identify, but it was the smell of burning flesh.
It was the crematorium.
There we were, really in the middle of the biggest death factory of modern civilization.

And our minds could not understand that one human being could be treated the way those women were treated just because she happened to be born with a different religion or maybe just had one Jewish grandparent.
We just couldn't understand.

And I think this is sort of the underlying story of this whole period, that nobody believed that 20th century, civilized Germany could behave the way they believed-- behaved, because this didn't happen in a third-world country.
It wasn't a backward African country where cannibalism was the order of the day maybe 50 years before.
This was very civilized Germany.
So I guess that was one of the reasons that we didn't believe that anything like this could happen.
We were taken into the so-called disinfectation center, the showers.
And as I was standing there waiting to get in, I turned around and I saw the men from our transport behind us.
I saw my father there.
And we were not allowed to talk to  each other, but with the silent movement of his lips, he asked, where is your mother?
I shrugged, of course.
I didn't know where my mother is.

But again, the only way I can understand and I can explain the absolute, totally desperate look in his eyes when he didn't see my mother with me is that he was one of the few people who had an idea what is going to the left meant.
And I guess this is not my imagination.
The prisoners that helped us off the train were some French, and some of German nationality.
And my father did speak both languages fluently, so somebody must have whispered something in his ears, because otherwise I wouldn't understand why he was so sad.
I wasn't, because I was going to see my mother later and my father was standing right behind me with a group of men.
We were taken into these showers.
And the first thing they did was, of course, we had to strip.
We had to take all our clothes off, which was the first traumatic experience, because your 18-year-old of 1944 is probably a lot younger than your 18-year-old of 1990s.
Walking among those staring prisoners and SS soldiers naked was pretty traumatic at that point.
Our heads were shaved.
And I do remember this.
I was with two of my-- three of my girlfriends.
And we looked at each other after our heads were shaved.
We could hardly recognize one another.
And I think it was sort of like some kind of a really hysterical laughter.
We just started to laugh like it was an uncontrollable thing.
I don't know.
It was the shock, probably.
We were given one piece of garment.
And I remember I had a blue-- some kind of miserable dress.
And we were given a pair of panties.
And that was about it.
We kept our shoes.
Not everybody did, but I think at that point, that group, we kept our shoes, but no stockings or anything and nothing else.
That was the three pieces, shoes, pair of panties, and a dress.
And we were taken into the camps.
One of the first things that they made us do was we were each given a postcard.
And we had to write that postcard to a non-Jewish neighbor or friend or schoolmate, whoever.
And we all had to write the same thing.
We had to write that we arrived to camp.
We are working.
We are together, meaning the families.
And we're doing well.
They did keep up this charade.
I mean, it didn't matter that it was June the 3rd of 1944.
And I don't suppose that even the most fanatical Nazi could have had any hopes still of winning the war, but they did keep it up.
We were taken into barracks where we slept.
Some of the barracks had nothing but a bare floor.
The one that I was taken to had three-story bunk beds.
They were double beds, nothing except the hard board.
No pillow, no straw, no mattress, no blanket, absolutely nothing.
Seven of us slept on one of these double beds.
The only way we could sleep was on our side.
If one person had to turn, of course everybody else had to.
We-- our-- the person responsible for each barracks, the so-called block elder, 'blockalteste,' was usually a woman from Poland or Slovakia.
These people have been in Auschwitz for years.
Actually, they built the camp.
Very few of them survived at that point, not because of gas chambers, but because of winters and hard labor and lack of everything.
Those who survived, it was the survival of the fittest, basically.
Those who survived were really hardened human beings.
They could not forgive us that we had a normal life while they were already suffering.
And we really felt that hatred very intensively.
They tried to tell us from the beginning that we'll never see our mothers or the children or anybody who we were separated from.
They pointed at the other side, and they said-- they showed that in Camp H for Heaven, 'himmel' in German.
First we didn't want to believe them, because we thought they're just mean and they just really hate us.
But, as time went by, nobody ever came back, so we started to believe that what they're telling us is the truth.
We had roll calls every morning as early as 3, 4 o'clock, and again later at night.
The roll calls were really not to count people, because escaping was impossible, the high voltage electricity in those barbed wires that surrounded us.
The roll calls were used for two purposes.
Our miserable food was given to us during roll calls, which consisted of some soup with some rotten vegetables floating in it.
And the way it was given to us, there was one pot, five people standing, five of them, and one behind the other.
No spoons, no individual dishes, nothing.
One pot and five people had to drink out of that one pot.
The same way we got some unsweetened liquid, that-- brown liquid that was called coffee.
Also one pot, five people drinking.
And we got a piece of bread, a square slice that had more sawdust than wheat.
But that was the only nutritious part of our-- nutritious part of our diet.
Every once in a while we would get a little piece of marmalade or some margarine with our bread.
And that was also given to us during roll calls.
Every once in a while they would give us like a little piece of wurst or some similar kind of thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were two things in Auschwitz, though.
But basically, these roll calls were used for selections.
People who didn't look fit, who didn't look good, which was no big accomplishment under the circumstances, were taken away.
And we soon learned that nobody ever came back.
There were two things in Auschwitz.
I think the most horrible thing was your total sense of isolation.
Auschwitz was-- Birkenau, the camps-- totally bare.
I mean, it was the dirt road, or the cement.
Not even a blade of glass-- grass of course.
You were totally isolated from the outside world.
At this point, there was nobody between you and your murderers.
There was no reason to shout, because nobody could hear you.
And also because of the constant selections there was a sense of hopelessness.
You just did not know one day whether you're going to survive the next day or not.
Also, Auschwitz was a society that had more differences, class differences, probably, than any normal society in-- in the free world or not-so-free world.
We knew that-- you know, I did not come from a classless society.
We knew that money mattered and that people belonged to different classes.
But that was nothing to prepare you for Auschwitz.
Those people who had some kind of power, like your block elder for instance, they stole probably half of your rations, if you got a little wurst or anything that was worthwhile stealing.
And they had some kind of a stove where they cooked meals from stuff that they stole from the prisoners.
And I remember once our bunk bed was right next to the block elder's room.
She was the only one who had a separate room.
And there was a crack on the wall.
These barracks were wooden barracks.
And we could see, and smell through that crack that-- what they were cooking up from the rations that they basically stole from us.
The difference there between a block elder and the rest of us was greater than between the poorest person and nobility in the world that we knew before.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were random selections, what-- which meant that they would close all the barracks from the outside.
We were all ordered to stay in.
And maybe after a couple of hours we would find out how many hundreds of people they took away for the gas chambers.
I think it was really just numbers.
They needed people to-- to feed the gas chambers.
After seven weeks under these circumstances I got very ill.
I had a high fever.
My friends tried to hide me behind somebody during roll calls.
But after about three days there was no way, because I had the measles and they were visible all over my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
OK.
Yeah.
OK.
I had one communication from my father.
We were, of course, not allowed to communicate.
That was punishable by death.
But somehow, the first week, I think maybe, during the first two weeks, we got some little wooden pieces, white wood that had something written on them thrown over the fence.
Somebody threw it over.
And I had one from my father saying that he sends love.
And, later on, I found out that my father left Auschwitz at the first opportunity.
I think he volunteered, actually, because they would take these people away for work.
And he volunteered.
I found out that later he left Auschwitz in June.
As I said, he was exceptionally bright and he just always knew what the right thing was to do.
But he didn't know enough to survive.
